Name: Angels at the Ascension
Also Known As: The Angels at Christ's Ascension; The Two Men in White Apparel; The Angelic Witnesses of the Ascension; The Ascension Proclamation; Men of Galilee
Scripture References: Acts 1:9–11; Luke 24:50–53; Mark 16:19; Daniel 7:13–14; Psalm 24:7–10 (LXX 23); Psalm 47:5 (LXX 46); Psalm 110:1 (LXX 109); Hebrews 4:14; Hebrews 9:24; Ephesians 4:8–10
Brief: The two heavenly messengers who appeared to the Apostles immediately after Christ’s Ascension from the Mount of Olives (Acts 1:9–11). As the disciples gazed upward, where a cloud had received the Lord from their sight, “two men stood by them in white apparel” — universally understood as angels — and said, “Men of Galilee, why do you stand gazing up into heaven? This same Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will so come in like manner as you saw Him go.” Unlike the angels at Bethlehem or the empty tomb, these angels announce no new event but interpret one already witnessed: they do not cause the Ascension or accompany Christ into heaven, but remain with the Apostles to explain His heavenly enthronement and proclaim the certainty of His return. Scripture names neither of them, and the Church keeps that silence. The two angels of the Ascension are the deliberate pair of the two angels at the empty tomb (HH-0036) — heaven’s witnesses at both the victory over death and the exaltation of human nature.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; New Testament; Acts of the Apostles; Ascension of Christ; Twelve Great Feasts; Angels; Heavenly Hosts; Second Coming; Pentecost; Mount of Olives; Apostles; Divine Exaltation; Heavenly Kingship; Salvation History; Eschatology; Angelic Proclamation; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: The eighth New-Testament Biblical Encounter, completing the sequence of angelic appearances around the principal mysteries of Christ’s ministry. Acts 1:10 gives “two men in white apparel,” universally understood as angels — a plurality, so entity type Collective; Celestial Order blank; not named as Gabriel or Michael, per Scripture and the dossier. Acts does NOT use the title “the angel of the Lord,” so this anchors to the Angels (HH-0009), and it is the deliberate typological PAIR of the two angels at the empty tomb (HH-0036) — the Fathers note the symmetry of two angels at the Resurrection and two at the Ascension, both witnessing and interpreting rather than causing God’s saving acts (the dossier’s central caution: interpreters, not participants — Christ ascends by His own authority). The heavenly hosts who RECEIVE the ascending King — the “Lift up your gates, O princes” of Psalm 24, read as a dialogue of the angelic ranks — are distinct from these two interpreter-angels, as the iconography itself distinguishes them, and are treated in the prose, not conflated. Register note: the Deuterocanonical column carries Sirach 48:9 (Elijah “taken up in a whirlwind of fire,” the deuterocanonical account of the great Old-Testament TYPE of the Ascension) and Wisdom of Solomon 5:15–16 (the righteous glorified, foreshadowing the exaltation of human nature); 2 Maccabees 2:7–8 is kept out as too thematic. Extra-biblically, the early-Christian Ascension of Isaiah genuinely narrates Christ’s ascent through the heavens with the angelic orders welcoming Him — but as a visionary apocryphon the Church does NOT rely upon — and 1 Enoch supplies the Son-of-Man-in-glory and heavenly-ascent background (Daniel 7); Jubilees, 2 Esdras, and the Testament writings are thematic only. No feast commemorates the two angels on their own; the Ascension is one of the Twelve Great Feasts (FF-0010, forty days after Pascha), and their words point on to Pentecost (FF-0011).

Name: The Seven Trumpet Angels
Also Known As: The Seven Angels with Trumpets; Seven Angels Before God; The Seven Trumpeting Angels; Trumpet Angels of Revelation; The Seven Angels Who Stand Before God
Scripture References: Revelation 8:2; Revelation 8:6–13; Revelation 9:1–12; Revelation 9:13–21; Revelation 11:15–19; Exodus 19:16–19; Numbers 10:1–10; Joshua 6:1–20; Joel 2:1; Zephaniah 1:14–16; Zechariah 9:14
Brief: The seven angels who stand before God and are given seven trumpets in the Apocalypse (Revelation 8–11). As each sounds his trumpet a judgment falls upon a portion of creation — earth, sea, rivers, the lights of heaven, then the demonic locusts of the abyss and the horsemen loosed at the Euphrates — until the seventh trumpet proclaims that “the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ” (Revelation 11:15). Orthodox tradition understands them as faithful ministers who execute God’s righteous judgments, never as independent powers: the judgments are God’s, the angels His obedient heralds. Their trumpets gather up the trumpets of Scripture — Sinai, Jericho, the Day of the Lord — and Revelation 8:2’s “the seven angels who stand before God” echoes the seven holy angels of Tobit and 1 Enoch, whom the tradition connects to the archangels. Scripture never names them, and the Church — which receives Revelation as canonical yet does not appoint it for liturgical reading — commemorates them on no feast.

Notes: The first of the Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounters (filed under the hub’s “The Book of Revelation” section — tagged “Book of Revelation”, NOT “New Testament”, so it does not double-render). Entity type Collective (seven angels); Celestial Order blank — Scripture assigns no rank and Orthodox theology declines to. The trumpet angels are NOT named and are not identified with a single archangel, but Revelation 8:2’s definite phrase “the seven angels who stand before God” deliberately evokes a known group: the seven holy angels of Tobit 12:15 (“one of the seven who present the prayers of the saints before the glory of the Holy One”) and 1 Enoch 20 (which names them) — the Second-Temple seven archangels, of whom Orthodox tradition venerates eight (Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, Uriel, Selaphiel, Jegudiel, Barachiel, Jeremiel). So the encounter cross-links to the Archangels (HH-0008) and, for the trumpet of judgment, to Michael (HH-0010, whose voice and the trumpet of God are joined in 1 Thessalonians 4:16); whether the seven trumpet angels ARE the seven archangels is left as labeled interpretation, not asserted. Register note: the Deuterocanonical column carries Tobit 12:15 (the seven who stand before God — the direct antecedent of Rev 8:2) and the Extra-Biblical column 1 Enoch (which names the seven archangels of the throne, 1 Enoch 20); the trumpet Old-Testament parallels (Sinai, Jericho, Joel’s Day of the Lord) are protocanonical and sit in Scripture References. Interpretation follows St. Andrew of Caesarea — the principal Orthodox commentator on the Apocalypse — reading the judgments as both symbolic and eschatological, the Church fixing no one literal correlation to historical events. A standing point for the whole section: the Book of Revelation is canonical Scripture yet is the one New-Testament book never appointed for reading in the Byzantine liturgical services, so these angels have no feast and appear only in Apocalypse iconography and in Last-Judgment imagery (cf. the Sunday of the Last Judgment / Meatfare, FF-0029). The Angel with the Golden Censer (Rev 8:3–5) and the Four Angels at the Euphrates (Rev 9:14) are distinct encounters, noted in the prose pending their own records.

Name: The Seven Bowl Angels
Also Known As: The Seven Angels with the Seven Bowls; Seven Angels with the Last Plagues; Angels of the Seven Bowls; Seven Vial Angels; The Bowls of Wrath
Scripture References: Revelation 15:1; Revelation 15:5–8; Revelation 16:1–21; Revelation 17:1; Revelation 19:9–10; Revelation 21:9; Revelation 22:8–9; Exodus 7–12; Exodus 25:29; 1 Kings 7:50; Isaiah 51:17; Ezekiel 38–39; Joel 3:1–16
Brief: The seven angels who come out of the heavenly temple bearing the seven golden bowls of the wrath of God, “the seven last plagues” (Revelation 15–16). At the command “Go and pour out the bowls,” each empties one upon the world — festering sores, the sea and the rivers turned to blood, the scorching sun, the darkening of the beast’s kingdom, the drying of the Euphrates, and the final earthquake that topples Babylon the Great — completing God’s judgments, so that His wrath is “finished” (15:1). Orthodox tradition understands them, like the trumpet angels, as faithful ministers who execute God’s righteous judgment and hold no authority of their own. The bowls openly reprise the plagues of Egypt, and the golden vessels that once held the prayers of the saints (Revelation 5:8) now pour out the answer to those prayers. Yet the judgment turns toward mercy: one of the seven bowl angels becomes John’s guide, showing him first the fall of Babylon (17:1) and then the New Jerusalem, the Bride of the Lamb (21:9) — and when John falls to worship him, refuses: “Worship God” (22:9).

Notes: The second Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounter (tagged “Book of Revelation”, NOT “New Testament”), the deliberate parallel septet to the Seven Trumpet Angels (HH-0038): both are seven unnamed angels executing successive divine judgments. Entity type Collective; Celestial Order blank. Register note: where the trumpet angels connect to the seven-archangels tradition (Rev 8:2 “the seven who stand before God” → Tobit / 1 Enoch), the bowl angels connect instead to the EGYPTIAN PLAGUES — the sores, the waters turned to blood, and the darkness of Revelation 16 openly reprise Exodus — so the Deuterocanonical column carries Wisdom of Solomon 11–19, the deuterocanonical meditation on the plagues as God’s judgment through the very elements. The Extra-Biblical column is deliberately left BLANK: unlike the trumpets, no apocalyptic text (1 Enoch and the like) uniquely parallels the seven bowls — their background is the plague tradition, not an apocalyptic septet — an honest blank, with 1 Enoch’s angels of punishment noted only as general atmosphere in the prose. Two theological points are foregrounded: (1) the “golden bowls” that held the prayers of the saints (Rev 5:8; cf. the golden censer of 8:3–5) become the bowls of wrath, the martyrs’ prayers answered in judgment; (2) one bowl angel becomes John’s guide from Babylon’s fall (17:1) to the New Jerusalem (21:9), and when John falls to worship him REFUSES — “Worship God” (19:10; 22:8–9) — the clearest scriptural witness that the angels are fellow servants and created messengers, not to be worshipped (the created-messenger thread running from the Angel of the Lord HH-0019 and the Commander of the Lord’s Army HH-0020). As with all the Revelation angels: no feast, no liturgical reading of the Apocalypse in the Byzantine services, St. Andrew of Caesarea the principal commentator, the judgments read symbolically and eschatologically without a fixed correlation to historical events.

Name: The Mighty Angel of Revelation 10
Also Known As: The Mighty Angel; The Strong Angel; The Angel with the Little Scroll; The Angel with the Little Book; The Mighty Messenger of Revelation
Scripture References: Revelation 10:1–4; Revelation 10:5–7; Revelation 10:8–11; Ezekiel 2:8–3:3; Daniel 12:5–7; Exodus 13:21; Genesis 9:12–17; Revelation 1:12–16; Revelation 4:3
Brief: “Another mighty angel” who comes down from heaven between the sixth and seventh trumpets (Revelation 10), clothed with a cloud, a rainbow over his head, his face like the sun and his feet like pillars of fire. Holding a little open scroll, he sets one foot upon the sea and the other upon the land, cries out with a great voice like a lion, and swears by the eternal Creator that there shall be “no more delay” before the mystery of God is finished. He gives John the scroll to eat — sweet as honey in the mouth, bitter in the stomach — recommissioning the seer to prophesy, in the joy of God’s revelation and the sorrow of the judgment it carries. His glory so resembles the descriptions of the risen Christ that some early writers read him as the Lord Himself, but the text calls him “another angel,” and the dominant Orthodox reading, following St. Andrew of Caesarea, holds him a created messenger who bears God’s authority without being divine by nature — leaving the figure, as Scripture does, without a name.

Notes: Third Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounter (tagged “Book of Revelation”, not “New Testament”). A SINGLE angel — entity type Scriptural Angel; Celestial Order blank; unnamed and not identified with Michael or Gabriel. The central register/interpretation point: the Mighty Angel’s appearance (cloud, rainbow, face like the sun, feet of fire) so resembles the risen Christ of Revelation 1 that SOME early Christian writers read him AS Christ — a Christophany — but the text calls him “another mighty angel” (Greek allon angelon ischyron), and the dominant Orthodox reading (St. Andrew of Caesarea) holds him a created angel bearing divine authority, not Christ. Presented as labeled interpretation, not settled — the same “is this the Lord, or His messenger?” question the Old Testament raises for the Angel of the Lord (HH-0019, cross-linked), here answered by most toward the created messenger. Register note: BOTH the Deuterocanonical and Extra-Biblical columns are left BLANK. The primary intertext is protocanonical — Ezekiel 2:8–3:3 (the prophet commanded to eat the scroll) and Daniel 12:7 (the angel’s oath that the end is fixed), both in Scripture References — and no deuterocanonical text parallels the figure; the closest extra-biblical parallel, 2 Esdras / 4 Ezra 14 (Ezra drinking a cup of inspiration and dictating the scriptures), is only a thematic ingestion-of-revelation motif, noted in the prose, not cited. The angel appears within the trumpet cycle (between the sixth and seventh), so it anchors alongside the Seven Trumpet Angels (HH-0038). No feast; the Apocalypse is not read in the Byzantine services; St. Andrew of Caesarea is the principal commentator, reading the vision symbolically and eschatologically.

Name: The Angel with the Golden Censer
Also Known As: The Angel at the Heavenly Altar; The Angel Offering Incense; The Incense Angel; The Angel Before the Golden Altar
Scripture References: Revelation 8:3–5; Revelation 5:8; Exodus 30:1–10; Leviticus 16:12–13; Psalm 141:2 (LXX 140); Isaiah 6:6–7; Ezekiel 10:2
Brief: “Another angel” who stands at the heavenly altar with a golden censer, seen just before the Seven Trumpet Angels sound (Revelation 8:3–5). He is given much incense to offer with the prayers of all the saints upon the golden altar before the throne, and the smoke of the incense rises before God together with those prayers; then he fills the censer with fire from the altar and casts it upon the earth, and there follow thunder, lightning, voices, and an earthquake — the prelude to the trumpet judgments. The scene binds heaven’s worship to God’s justice: the prayers of the faithful are received into the heavenly liturgy, and from the same altar the judgments proceed. His ministry takes up the priestly incense-offering of the Temple (Exodus 30), and Orthodox worship still lifts incense as the sign of prayer ascending — “Let my prayer arise as incense before You” (Psalm 141:2). Scripture gives him no name; the tradition sees a faithful heavenly minister, not a named archangel, though his office of presenting the saints’ prayers is exactly that ascribed to the Archangel Raphael in the Book of Tobit.

Notes: Fourth Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounter (tagged “Book of Revelation”, not “New Testament”). A single “another angel” — entity type Scriptural Angel; Celestial Order blank; unnamed, not a named archangel. It stands at a hinge of the Apocalypse: Rev 8:3–5 (the golden censer) immediately PRECEDES and triggers the Seven Trumpet Angels (HH-0038, cross-linked) — the fire from the altar cast to earth begins the trumpet judgments — and the “prayers of the saints” in the golden censer are the same prayers whose golden vessels become the bowls of wrath in the Seven Bowl Angels (HH-0039, cross-linked; Rev 5:8). Register note: the Deuterocanonical column carries Tobit 12:12, 15 — Raphael’s own words, “I brought the reminder of your prayer before the Holy One… I am one of the seven who present the prayers of the saints and enter before the glory of the Holy One” — the direct antecedent of this angel’s prayer-presenting office (closer here than at the trumpets, because Rev 8:3–4 is specifically about presenting prayers); so the record also cross-links to the Archangel Raphael (HH-0012). The Extra-Biblical column carries 1 Enoch, where the holy angels bear the petitions of the righteous before the Lord (1 Enoch 9; 40; 99). The Old-Testament incense/altar parallels (Exodus 30; Leviticus 16; Psalm 141; Isaiah 6; Ezekiel 10) are protocanonical and sit in Scripture References. This is the most liturgically resonant of the Revelation angels: though the Apocalypse is never read in the Byzantine services, the incense-as-prayer imagery of Rev 8 is woven through Orthodox worship (the censing of the church; “Let my prayer arise as incense,” Psalm 141, at Vespers), so the liturgical-tradition section is fuller here than for the other Revelation encounters. No feast of its own; St. Andrew of Caesarea the principal commentator.

Name: Michael and the War in Heaven
Also Known As: The War in Heaven; Michael's Victory over the Dragon; Michael and the Dragon; The Heavenly Battle; The Casting Down of Satan
Scripture References: Revelation 12:7–12; Daniel 10:13; Daniel 10:21; Daniel 12:1; Jude 9; Jude 6; Luke 10:18; Isaiah 14:12–15; Ezekiel 28:11–19; Psalm 103:20–21 (LXX 102); 2 Peter 2:4
Brief: The War in Heaven (Revelation 12:7–12): “Michael and his angels fought against the dragon; and the dragon and his angels fought, but they did not prevail.” The great dragon — “that serpent of old, called the Devil and Satan” — is cast down from heaven with his angels, losing his place as “the accuser of our brethren” before God’s throne. Orthodox tradition honors Michael here as the Archistrategos, the Commander of the heavenly hosts, whose defense of God’s kingdom continues the role Scripture gives him in Daniel and Jude. Yet the vision is careful that the victory is not the angels’ own: the saints “overcame him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony” (12:11) — the dragon is defeated through Christ. The Church reads the battle not as a chronicle of cosmic history but as the revealing of a spiritual reality: Satan’s decisive defeat through Christ’s death, resurrection, and ascension, and the continuing struggle of the Church, whom the fallen dragon still assails on earth.

Notes: Fifth Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounter, and the special case flagged earlier: it centers on the NAMED Archangel Michael (HH-0010), not an unnamed angel. Handled by the established pattern — the being (Michael, HH-0010) keeps his own record; the EVENT (the War in Heaven, Rev 12:7–12) is a distinct encounter cross-linked to him — exactly as the Annunciations (HH-0030/0031) are Gabriel-events distinct from Gabriel-the-being (HH-0011), and as Daniel’s Angelic Visions (HH-0029) is a Michael/Gabriel event. Entity type Collective (Michael AND his angels vs. the dragon AND his angels — a battle of hosts, like HH-0029); Celestial Order blank (several orders; Michael’s own rank is carried on HH-0010). Central theology (the dossier’s caution): Michael is commander, but the victory is CHRIST’s — “they overcame him by the blood of the Lamb” (12:11) — the angels are not equal powers in a dualistic war; Satan is a defeated creature. Michael’s continuity is threaded: Daniel 10–12 (the prince who defends God’s people, cf. HH-0029), Jude 9 (disputing with the devil over the body of Moses — where Michael does NOT rebuke Satan on his own authority but says “The Lord rebuke you,” the model of angelic humility), and Rev 12. Register note: the Deuterocanonical column carries Wisdom of Solomon 2:24 (“through the envy of the devil death entered the world” — the deuterocanonical witness to the adversary cast down); the Extra-Biblical column carries 1 Enoch (where Michael binds the fallen watchers, 1 Enoch 10, and which Jude quotes directly, Jude 14–15) and the Assumption of Moses (the pseudepigraphon that is the source of Jude 9’s dispute between Michael and the devil over Moses’ body — a scholarly point handled openly). Isaiah 14:12–15 and Ezekiel 28:11–19, the traditional background for Satan’s fall, are PROTOcanonical (Scripture References), but their application to the devil is LATER Christian interpretation — Ezekiel 28 was originally addressed to the king of Tyre — so they are framed as labeled interpretation, not asserted as literal. Unlike the other Revelation angels, this event IS commemorated liturgically: Michael’s imagery from Rev 12 pervades the hymns and icons of the Synaxis of the Archangel Michael and the Other Bodiless Powers (Nov 8, FF-0071), even though the Apocalypse itself is not read in the services. The dragon and his fallen angels belong to the fallen-angels dimension of the database (in scope, marked), whose fuller treatment awaits its own records.

Name: The Four Angels at the Euphrates
Also Known As: The Four Bound Angels; The Four Angels Bound at the Euphrates; The Angels of the Sixth Trumpet; The Euphrates Angels
Scripture References: Revelation 9:13–15; Revelation 9:16–21; Genesis 15:18; Isaiah 8:7–8; Jeremiah 46:2–10; Daniel 10:13; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6
Brief: The four angels “bound at the great river Euphrates,” released at the sounding of the sixth trumpet (Revelation 9:13–21). A voice from the golden altar before God commands the sixth trumpet-angel to loose them; once freed at the appointed “hour and day and month and year,” they lead an immense mounted army that kills a third of humanity — and still, the vision laments, the survivors do not repent of their idolatry and violence. Alone among Revelation’s angels these four are bound, and Scripture does not say why: whether they are holy angels held back for a set mission, or fallen powers whose destruction God restrains until His hour. The tradition, following St. Andrew of Caesarea, does not resolve the question — it leaves the text’s silence intact. What the passage does declare is God’s sovereignty: bound or loosed, holy or fallen, these angels act only at His command, at the moment He appoints, and within the limits He sets.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Book of Revelation; Angels; Heavenly Hosts; Divine Judgment; Sixth Trumpet; Euphrates; Bound Angels; Fallen Angels; Apocalypse; Eschatology; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: Sixth Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounter (tagged “Book of Revelation”). Four angels — entity type Collective; Celestial Order blank. The defining register/interpretation point: these are the ONLY angels in Revelation described as “bound” (9:14), and Scripture does not say whether they are holy angels temporarily restrained for a future mission or fallen/demonic powers whose destruction God limits — a genuine ambiguity that St. Andrew of Caesarea and the Orthodox tradition deliberately leave OPEN. This record honors that: both readings are presented as labeled interpretation, NEITHER asserted, and the encounter is filed without resolving it to “holy” or “fallen.” The “bound until an appointed hour” motif is exactly that of the fallen watchers of 1 Enoch 10 (bound until the day of judgment) and the angels “kept in chains until the judgment” of 2 Peter 2:4 and Jude 6 (protocanonical, in Scripture References) — which is why 1 Enoch is carried in the Extra-Biblical column; the Deuterocanonical column is left blank, since the Euphrates-judgment parallels (Genesis 15, Isaiah 8, Jeremiah 46) are all protocanonical. Tightly bound to the trumpet cycle: these are the angels of the SIXTH trumpet, so the record anchors to the Seven Trumpet Angels (HH-0038); the command to release them comes “from the four horns of the golden altar” (9:13) — the altar of the Angel with the Golden Censer (HH-0041, cross-linked) — and the sixth BOWL too falls at the Euphrates (Rev 16:12), linking the Seven Bowl Angels (HH-0039). The Euphrates carries its own weight: the boundary of the Promised Land (Genesis 15:18) and the direction of Israel’s invaders (Assyria, Babylon), now the source of a divinely permitted judgment. As with all the Revelation angels: no feast, the Apocalypse not read in the services, and the Church cautions against tying the four to modern nations or geopolitical events. The Four Angels at the four corners of the earth (Rev 7:1) are a DISTINCT encounter, noted in the prose pending their own record.

Name: The Four Angels of the Four Corners of the Earth
Also Known As: The Four Angels Holding the Four Winds; The Four Angels of Revelation 7; The Angels of the Four Winds; The Four Angels at the Four Corners
Scripture References: Revelation 7:1–3; Ezekiel 9:4–6; Jeremiah 49:36; Daniel 7:2; Zechariah 6:1–8
Brief: The four angels who stand “at the four corners of the earth,” holding back the four winds so that no wind blows upon earth, sea, or tree (Revelation 7:1–3). Their scene is a pause after the sixth seal: before any further judgment falls, another angel ascends from the east bearing the seal of the living God and commands the four not to harm creation “till we have sealed the servants of our God on their foreheads.” So the four angels restrain destruction — not to withhold judgment forever, but to hold it back until God’s faithful are marked and made safe. The “four corners” and “four winds” are the ancient biblical way of naming the whole world, not a map of its shape; the vision proclaims God’s authority over all creation and His mercy toward His own. Orthodox tradition sees in these four faithful ministers both God’s power over the forces of nature and His providential care: judgment is never arbitrary, and it waits upon the sealing of the saints. They are the mirror of the four bound angels of the Euphrates (Revelation 9) — one quartet loosing judgment, this one holding it back.

Notes: Seventh Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounter (tagged “Book of Revelation”). Four HOLY angels — entity type Collective; Celestial Order blank. Deliberate counterpart to the Four Angels at the Euphrates (HH-0043, cross-linked): Revelation gives two quartets of “four angels,” and they are opposites — the Euphrates four (Rev 9) are BOUND and LOOSE judgment (their nature left ambiguous), while these four (Rev 7) are faithful ministers who RESTRAIN destruction in mercy until the servants of God are sealed. That contrast is drawn out in the prose. The scene’s heart is the sealing (God protects and marks His own before judgment), whose direct Old-Testament type is Ezekiel 9:4–6 (the mark on the foreheads of the faithful before Jerusalem’s judgment) — protocanonical, in Scripture References, along with the four-winds imagery of Jeremiah 49:36, Daniel 7:2, and Zechariah 6:1–8. Register note: the Deuterocanonical column is left blank (the strong parallels are protocanonical); the Extra-Biblical column carries 1 Enoch, whose cosmology sets angels over the winds and the four quarters (1 Enoch 60; 76). Interpretation follows §9: the “four corners” / “four winds” are biblical idiom for the whole inhabited world, NOT a literal cosmology, and the Church cautions against tying the four to nations or geopolitics (St. Andrew of Caesarea the principal commentator). The sealing angel “ascending from the east” with the seal of God (Rev 7:2) is a DISTINCT figure, noted in the prose pending its own record. As with all the Revelation angels: no feast, the Apocalypse not read in the Byzantine services.

Name: The Angel Flying in Midheaven
Also Known As: The Angel with the Eternal Gospel; The Midheaven Angel; The First Angel of Revelation 14; The Angel Proclaiming the Eternal Gospel
Scripture References: Revelation 14:6–7; Revelation 14:8–11; Isaiah 40:9; Isaiah 52:7; Psalm 96:2–10 (LXX 95); Daniel 4:34–35; Matthew 24:14
Brief: The angel whom John sees “flying in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach to those who dwell on the earth — to every nation, tribe, tongue, and people” (Revelation 14:6–7). With a loud voice he calls all humanity to “fear God and give glory to Him, for the hour of His judgment has come,” and to worship the Creator of heaven and earth. He is the first of three angels of Revelation 14; the two who follow announce the fall of Babylon and warn against worshiping the beast. Unlike most of the Apocalypse’s angels this one is not an agent of judgment but a herald — his message a last, universal invitation to repentance even as the judgment nears. The “eternal gospel” is no new revelation but the timeless proclamation of God’s kingdom and His claim upon every creature; Orthodox reading sees the angel reinforcing, not replacing, the Church’s own preaching of the Gospel to all nations. Flying at “midheaven,” the zenith of the sky, he proclaims to the whole world at once. Scripture gives him no name, and the tradition assigns him none.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Book of Revelation; Angels; Heavenly Hosts; Eternal Gospel; Angelic Proclamation; Universal Mission; Divine Judgment; Repentance; Heavenly Worship; Apocalypse; Eschatology; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: Eighth Book-of-Revelation Biblical Encounter (tagged “Book of Revelation”). A SINGLE angel — entity type Scriptural Angel; Celestial Order blank; unnamed, not identified with Michael or Gabriel. A HERALD, not an agent of judgment — the register contrast with the trumpet/bowl/Euphrates angels: he proclaims the “eternal gospel,” a final universal call to repentance and worship before the judgments fall. He is the FIRST of the three angels of Revelation 14 (the second announces Babylon’s fall, 14:8; the third warns against the beast, 14:9–11), noted in the prose; the two others may take their own records. Interpretation (per §9): the “eternal gospel” is NOT a new or different gospel but the unchanging proclamation of God’s kingdom — Orthodoxy reads the angel as reinforcing, not replacing, the Church’s preaching (cf. Matt 24:14, the gospel preached to all nations before the end); “midheaven” (Greek mesouranēma) is the zenith of the sky, signing the universal reach of the message. Register note: BOTH the Deuterocanonical and Extra-Biblical columns are left BLANK — the parallels are protocanonical (the herald of good tidings, Isaiah 40:9 and 52:7; the call of all nations to worship the Creator-Judge, Psalm 96; God’s universal sovereignty, Daniel 4), and no deuterocanonical or extra-biblical text specifically parallels this figure. Cross-links: the Angels (HH-0009); the Seven Trumpet Angels (HH-0038), since Rev 14 stands within the Apocalypse’s cycle of judgment; and — a genuine thematic tie — the Nativity and the Heavenly Host (HH-0033), whose angels proclaimed “good tidings of great joy… to all people” at the Gospel’s beginning as this angel proclaims the “eternal gospel to every nation” near its end. As with all the Revelation angels: no feast, the Apocalypse not read in the Byzantine services; St. Andrew of Caesarea the principal commentator.

Name: Abaddon / Apollyon
Also Known As: Abaddon; Apollyon; The Destroyer; Angel of the Bottomless Pit; Angel of the Abyss; King of the Locusts
Scripture References: Revelation 9:1–11; Revelation 20:1–3; Job 26:6; Job 28:22; Job 31:12; Psalm 88:11 (LXX 87); Proverbs 15:11; Proverbs 27:20; Joel 2:1–11; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6; Luke 8:31
Brief: Abaddon (Hebrew, “Destruction”), called Apollyon in Greek (“Destroyer”), named in Revelation 9:11 as “the angel of the bottomless pit” — the king of the demonic locusts loosed from the abyss at the fifth trumpet. He is presented not as a faithful minister but as a ruler of destructive powers; yet even his authority is not his own. The abyss is opened only when the fallen star is “given” the key, the locusts may torment only those who lack the seal of God, and only for five months — every limit set by God. Orthodox Christianity reads the passage above all as a witness to God’s sovereignty: even the powers of destruction cannot act apart from His permission. Scripture does not identify Abaddon with Satan, nor with any other named demon, and the Church declines to add what the text withholds. The Hebrew word abaddon appears in the Old Testament as a name for the realm of destruction and the grave — an impersonal place; Revelation alone makes it the title of a personal spirit. A fallen being, catalogued here for study, not venerated.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Fallen; Abaddon; Apollyon; Fallen Angels; Demons; Abyss; Fifth Trumpet; Apocalypse; Divine Judgment; Spiritual Warfare; Eschatology; Orthodox Demonology; Bottomless Pit; Book of Revelation Figure
Notes: The first FALLEN being in the database (entity type Fallen), and the first entry in the hub’s new “The Fallen” section — set apart with a muted, marked styling and the standing note that such beings are catalogued for completeness and study, NOT venerated; the record carries no liturgical address. Tagged “Biblical Encounter; Fallen” (the “Fallen” section tag) and deliberately NOT “Book of Revelation” — so it files under “The Fallen” only, not among the holy-angel Revelation encounters (a record renders in every section whose tag it carries). Celestial Order blank: St. Dionysius the Areopagite excludes the fallen from the celestial hierarchy, so Abaddon belongs to no order of the Nine. Canonical Status Scriptural (Rev 9:11 names him). Key register points: (1) Abaddon is NOT Satan — Revelation does not identify him with Satan, Beelzebul, or any other named demon, and Orthodox tradition refuses to assert identities beyond the text; (2) the Old-Testament “abaddon” (Job 26:6; 28:22; 31:12; Proverbs 15:11; 27:20; Psalm 88:11) is an IMPERSONAL name for the realm of destruction / the grave — Revelation UNIQUELY personifies it as a spirit-king, and the two must not be conflated (the Old-Testament usage is kept protocanonical in Scripture References, not read back as the demon); (3) even the demonic acts only by God’s permission — the abyss is opened when the fallen star is “given” the key, the locusts may harm only the unsealed and only for five months, every limit God’s — the Orthodox refusal of dualism (St. John of Damascus: demons are fallen angels who retain intelligence but misuse their freedom, yet remain wholly subject to God). Register note: the Deuterocanonical column is left blank (the abaddon parallels are protocanonical; no deuterocanon names the figure); the Extra-Biblical column carries 1 Enoch, whose tradition of the abyss as the prison of the fallen (1 Enoch 10; 18–21) underlies Revelation’s “bottomless pit,” while 2 Peter 2:4 and Jude 6 (angels chained in the abyss) and Luke 8:31 (the demons begging not to be sent there) are protocanonical, in Scripture References. Within the trumpet cycle (his locusts are the fifth-trumpet judgment), so cross-linked to the Seven Trumpet Angels (HH-0038); linked to Michael and the War in Heaven (HH-0042) for the fallen / spiritual-warfare context, though Abaddon is NOT identified with the dragon/Satan there, and to the Four Angels at the Euphrates (HH-0043) for the shared abyss/bound-powers theme. The Church cautions against identifying Abaddon with historical rulers or modern figures. No feast — a demon is not commemorated; linked only to the Sunday of the Last Judgment (FF-0029), where the demonic powers meet their end (Rev 20).
